sentence: Subjective:
The patient, a 67-year-old male, presents with a history of essential hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and osteoarthritis.
He has been experiencing elevated and fluctuating blood pressures, with upper numbers occasionally over 180.
He reports feeling lightheaded and experiencing slight headaches, and has checked his blood pressure during these episodes, which have been high.
He denies any recent fever, nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea.
He reports some chest heaviness but no sustained chest pain or respiratory difficulty.
He experiences unsteadiness and slight headaches when his blood pressure is higher, and feels lightheaded but not like he might pass out.
He is taking lisinopril 20mg twice daily for hypertension, and atorvastatin for hyperlipidemia.
He uses a pillbox and a new app to track his medication schedule, which he reports is mostly accurate.
He has a grandson who helped him set up the app and is a great help.
His grandson's softball team recently had a good tournament, and he is currently able to golf and walk the full course, with some modifications due to weather changes.
He takes Tylenol for mild pain and adjusts his activity level as needed.
Objective:
Vital signs: Not mentioned
Physical exam: The patient has a grade 3/6 systolic ejection murmur, mild bibasilar rales, and trace lower extremity edema bilaterally.
Test Results: Not mentioned
Assessment / Problem List:
The patient has elevated blood pressure, hyperlipidemia, and osteoarthritis.
Elevated blood pressure; Hyperlipidemia; Osteoarthritis; Active.
Plan:
I increased the patient's lisinopril to 60mg twice daily and added 25mg of hydrochlorothiazide daily to help control his blood pressure.
I instructed him to check his blood pressure twice daily for the next two weeks.
I also sent blood tests including a cholesterol panel, CBC with differential, comprehensive metabolic panel, and urinalysis to check his kidney function.
I continued the current management plan for his hyperlipidemia with atorvastatin.
For his osteoarthritis, I advised him to continue to stay active, avoid overdoing it, and take Tylenol for mild pain.
I instructed him to see me back in two weeks.
Follow-up:
The patient will see me in two weeks for a follow-up appointment.
